kept.text:
🧵 Day 19/30 — #SystemDesign

Service Discovery: How microservices find each other without hardcoding URLs
In a simple app, services talk using fixed URLs:
→ https://t.co/jcvl4XSo6R → https://t.co/gh7UH6d25g

But in real systems, instances scale up/down dynamically. Containers restart. IPs change. New instances spin up during traffic spikes.

So… how does one service know where another service is running right now?
That’s where Service Discovery comes in.
Service discovery is a mechanism that helps services dynamically find and communicate with each other without hardcoding addresses.
 
⸻
 
The Problem Without It :

Imagine 10 instances of Payment Service.
→ New instances added → Some instances crash → IPs constantly change
If services rely on fixed addresses:
→ Requests fail → Routing breaks → Manual updates needed → System becomes fragile
This doesn’t scale.
 
⸻
 
How It Works :
Each service instance:
→ Registers itself with a Service Registry → Shares its IP, port, health status
When another service wants to call it:
→ It queries the registry → Gets a list of healthy instances → Sends request to one of them
Flow:
Service A → Registry → Service B instance
Dynamic and reliable.
 
⸻
 
Two Main Approaches :
Client-Side Discovery
→ Client queries registry directly → Client decides which instance to call
Examples:
→ Netflix Eureka → Consul
More control, but adds complexity to client.
 
⸻
 
Server-Side Discovery
→ Client sends request to load balancer → Load balancer queries registry → Routes request automatically
Examples:
→ Kubernetes Services → AWS ELB
Simpler for clients.
 
⸻
 
Why It Matters
→ Handles dynamic scaling → Improves fault tolerance → Enables auto-recovery → Removes hardcoded dependencies → Supports containerized environments → Essential for microservices
Without it, microservices become tightly coupled again.
 
⸻
 
Real-World Usage
→ Kubernetes uses built-in service discovery via DNS → Netflix uses Eureka for dynamic discovery → AWS uses internal service routing → Consul used in many distributed systems
Modern cloud systems rely heavily on this.
 
⸻
 
Health Checks (Critical)
Registry doesn’t just store instances.
It tracks:
→ Is instance alive? → Is it responding properly? → Should it receive traffic?
Unhealthy instances are removed automatically.
 
⸻
 
Challenges Most Ignore
→ Registry itself becomes critical component → Needs high availability → Consistency of service list → Cache vs real-time updates → Network partitions affect discovery
Service discovery must be reliable or entire system suffers.

#30DaysOfSystemDesign #ServiceDiscovery #BackendEngineering

🧵 Day 18/30 — #SystemDesign

Distributed Locks: How systems prevent race conditions at scale

Two users try to book the last seat at the same time.
Two workers try to process the same payment job.
Two services update the same inventory record simultaneously.

Without coordination, you get duplicate actions, inconsistent data, and chaos.

That’s why systems use Distributed Locks.

A distributed lock ensures that only one process at a time can access or modify a shared resource across multiple servers.

⸻

Simple Example

Ticket booking system:

→ Only 1 seat left
→ User A and User B click “Book” at same time

Without lock:

→ Both requests processed
→ Seat sold twice ❌

With distributed lock:

→ First request acquires lock
→ Second request waits / fails
→ Only one booking succeeds ✅

Consistency preserved.

⸻

How It Works

Flow:

→ Process requests lock for resource
→ If lock available → granted
→ If locked → wait / retry / fail
→ After operation → lock released

Lock must be:

→ Fast
→ Reliable
→ Automatically released (if process crashes)

⸻

Where It’s Used

→ Payment processing (avoid double charge)
→ Inventory management
→ Job schedulers (cron jobs)
→ Leader election in clusters
→ Cache updates
→ File processing systems

Anywhere shared state exists across nodes.

⸻

Common Implementations

Redis (SETNX + Expiry)
→ Simple and widely used
→ Needs careful handling

Zookeeper / etcd
→ Strong consistency
→ Used in critical systems

Database locks
→ Row-level locking
→ Slower but reliable

Distributed lock libraries (Redlock, etc.)
→ Higher-level abstraction

⸻

Challenges Most Ignore

Distributed locks are tricky:

→ Deadlocks if not released
→ Lock expiry timing issues
→ Network delays causing false locks
→ Split-brain scenarios
→ Performance overhead

Wrong implementation can break system more than help.

⸻

Important Concept: Lock Expiry

If process crashes after acquiring lock:

→ Lock should auto-expire
→ Otherwise resource stays blocked forever

Always use TTL with locks.

⸻

Real Companies

→ Payment systems ensure single transaction execution
→ Booking systems prevent double reservations
→ Distributed schedulers ensure one leader runs jobs
→ Fintech apps use locks for balance updates

⸻

Golden Rule

Use locks only when necessary.

Prefer idempotency + database constraints first.
Locks are powerful but add complexity.

#30DaysOfSystemDesign #DistributedSystems #BackendEngineering

TREASUREX MAKES EVERY DISCOVERY MEANINGFUL!!

One of the most interesting aspects of TreasureX is how it changes the way people see everyday environments. What once looked ordinary becomes part of a larger, connected system where real-world objects can carry digital value and purpose.

In daily life, most people walk past things like kiosks, vending machines, charging stations, or public devices without giving them much thought. They are seen as normal infrastructure, useful but unnoticed. 

#TreasureX shifts this perspective by showing that these same objects can become active components of a secure, verifiable on-chain ecosystem.

This is not just about technology. It is about awareness. Participants are encouraged to observe their surroundings more carefully and understand how physical spaces can be mapped, verified, and connected to digital networks. Through this process, discovery becomes intentional rather than accidental.

Importantly, participation is not limited to earning rewards. The deeper value lies in learning. Every action, whether it is mapping a device or verifying its existence, contributes to building practical knowledge and real-world Web3 understanding.

 Over time, users develop skills in observation, validation, and digital interaction with physical infrastructure.

This creates a stronger link between Web3 systems and real-world adoption. Instead of remaining abstract or purely digital, blockchain utility becomes something that interacts directly with everyday life. 

Users begin to see how trust can be built through verification and transparency rather than assumption.

As engagement continues, participants naturally start to notice opportunities that were previously invisible.

 Small details in the environment begin to matter more. A simple device becomes part of a wider network of value and information.

In the long term, this approach transforms simple actions into meaningful contributions. What starts as casual participation develops into consistent involvement, learning, and real-world impact. 

@ShareX_Network ultimately shows that value is not only created in large actions but also in small, consistent discoveries that connect people, places, and technology practically.

quoted_tweet.text:
Introducing PeptAI

A fleet of autonomous agents for peptide drug discovery.

PeptAI removes the handoffs between discovery, synthesis, and wet-lab validation, the parts that slow most pipelines down.

How it works:

> Runs a 9-gate  pipeline (8 computational gates and 1 wet-lab gate) 24/7
> Publishes every gate decision openly and in real time via @Molecule_sci Labs
> Automatically pays from its own wallet for wet-lab experiments (SPR validation via @adaptyvbio) when gate milestones are reached
> Learns from wet-lab results and refines candidates across multiple runs

The fleet:

→ Agent-01: GLP-1R, metabolic health: 35 candidates advancing
→ Agent-02: KISS1R, fertility: 2 of 10 advanced to G9
→ Agent-03: OX2R, ADHD: lead candidate ready for wet lab
→ Agent-04: community-selected target: queued

The fleet scales with budget. Each agent runs the same 9-gate pipeline against its receptor, 24/7.

There are plenty of AI drug discovery pipelines. Most still have humans deciding which candidates advance. PeptAI's agent makes those calls itself, gate by gate. Candidates have to clear 8 computational gates to earn a wet-lab recommendation. Depending on the gate, the candidate is redesigned or discarded. Every decision is published on-chain.

PeptAI runs on a stack of open scientific tools and protocols:

> BIOS @bioaidevs: knowledge layer for literature search and novelty checks. When an agent needs to know what's been discovered or what the literature says about a target, it calls BIOS.
> Litefold @try_litefold: molecular dynamics for conformational stability across G5
> Adaptyv Bio @adaptyvbio: wet-lab synthesis and SPR validation at G9
> Molecule Labs @Molecule_sci: on-chain publication of every gate decision in real time
> @base: x402 infrastructure for machine-to-machine payments

What you can do with PeptAI:

• Follow along here for gate decisions, candidates, and updates as the pipeline runs

• Use the open methodology to benchmark your own pipeline against the gates

PeptAI is experimental and actively evolving. Gates, tools, and thresholds change as it learns.

Discover PeptAI now: https://t.co/qfdL6YDRqc

Project details: @peptai_

Current peptide drug discovery is slow and expensive, bottlenecked by coordination costs between discovery, synthesis, and CRO partners.

PeptAI deploys autonomous agents that run the full discovery pipeline and commission wet-lab experiments directly, eliminating the procurement and scheduling overhead.

Each runs continuously against a single receptor, aiming to open a new path in autonomous biotech R&D with far-reaching IP opportunities.

• Operates a 9-gate pipeline (8 computational gates + 1 wet-lab gate) on peptide candidates, 24/7
• Every gate decision published in real time via @Molecule_sci Labs
• When promising candidates clear key gates, funds release automatically to commission wet-lab experiments (SPR validation at @adaptyvbio).
• Learns from results and refines candidates across runs

So @vrilpeptides has three official partners so far: 

1. @Peptiderx_ $PEPTIDE

Vril focuses on simplifying peptide R&D as a DeSci accelerator, while PeptideRx brings tools for research and discovery. Together, they lower the barrier to peptide experimentation and make the process more accessible.

2. @EluentBio $ELUENT 

A cryptographically verifiable bioinformatics pipeline for peptides, covering sequence, structure, interactions, and origin. This adds onchain proof and transparency to Vril’s peptide products.

3. @peptome_ai $PEPT 

A decentralized health tracking app integrating wearables and real-world data. It acts as a “human biology intelligence layer,” allowing Vril’s personalized peptide stacks (32+ biomarkers) to connect directly with user data.

Why this matters:

• Full DeSci peptide stack
Research (PeptideRx) → Verification (EluentBio) → Application (Peptome AI)
A complete onchain pipeline, solving transparency and trust issues in traditional biotech.

• Strong synergy
Verifiable peptides + real-time health data + AI optimization
This creates a highly defensible, data-driven personalized health system.

• Massive upside
All three are early-stage onchain biotech projects. 

Vril isn’t just building a product, it’s helping define the infrastructure layer for DeSci peptides.

📍 Generative AI search is not a new channel. It is a shift in who controls discovery.

As recent analysis highlights, a growing share of users already rely on generative AI for search, with significantly higher adoption among younger cohorts. This is not just behavior change. It is a redistribution of how information is filtered and prioritized.

1️⃣ Authority Shift: Discovery moves from platforms and SEO logic to AI systems that synthesize and rank answers. Control over visibility shifts from ranking positions to model outputs.

2️⃣ Governance Gap: Most organizations optimize for traditional search but lack strategy for AI-mediated discovery. Content exists, but influence inside AI responses is not actively managed.

3️⃣ Strategic Risk: As reliance on AI search grows, brands without representation in AI-generated answers risk silent exclusion from consideration sets.

This is why many digital strategies scale traffic but lose relevance in AI-driven journeys.

The real challenge is not being searchable. It is being selected by systems that decide on behalf of the user.

“Baking soda, interestingly, very often does provide what the tumor lacks to be able to heal, but carbon dioxide is what the baking soda is providing, sodium plus carbon dioxide. And in the 18th century, people were already experimenting....

...The discovery of carbonated water was closely related to the observation that carbon dioxide helps with cancer, and people were already seeing great improvement, for example, in breast cancer just by exposing it to carbon dioxide.

There's a book that reviews the history of treating cancer with carbon dioxide, for example, carbon dioxide enemas, or breathing carbon dioxide, or sitting in a bag full of carbon dioxide to absorb it through the skin.

When you're under stress, the characteristic feature of cancer identified by Otto Warburg a hundred years ago is that it can't stop producing lactic acid, which is normal in an injury, but the injured tissue producing lactic acid stimulates its own growth...which is good for the healing process, but at some point the lactic acid has to stop to allow maturing of the tissue rather than continued growth.

Carbon dioxide is the feature produced by the respiring mitochondria, which should turn off lactic acid, but if something is limiting your ability to produce and retain carbon dioxide, then you can't turn off lactic acid.

And Simoncini's baking soda sometimes provided enough sodium and bicarbonate or carbon dioxide to suppress the lactic acid and allow healing to proceed.

[And do we know why this carbon dioxide process sometimes lowered?] 

Low thyroid, low calcium, low vitamin D, low sodium are all factors limiting the ability to make enough carbon dioxide.” — Ray Peat

quoted_tweet.text:
Baking soda slows the spread of some cancers.

In experiments, researchers gave mice sodium bicarbonate, also known as baking soda, by mouth. In mouse models of metastatic breast cancer, this reduced the number of spontaneous metastases, which are new tumors that form naturally when cancer cells spread from the original tumor to other parts of the body.

Sodium bicarbonate reduced the rate of cancer spread to lymph nodes, but did not reduce the number of circulating tumor cells. 

Sodium bicarbonate reduced liver metastases after cancer cells were injected into the spleen. This suggests it may have made it harder for cancer cells to leave blood vessels, settle in organs, and grow there.

The results were mixed in other cancer models. Sodium bicarbonate reduced metastases from one prostate cancer cell line, but not from melanoma cells.

Ref: Bicarbonate Increases Tumor pH and Inhibits Spontaneous Metastases

tweet.text:
my paper titled "Interpretable Discovery of One parameter Subgroups" has been accepted at #ICML2026!!

a few points on what the paper is about:
-> in geometric deep learning, you typically need to know the symmetry group upfront. in real scientific data, you often don't.
-> so we build a modular framework that discovers the underlying continuous symmetry subgroup directly from data. it covers all three geometric regimes: elliptical (rotations), hyperbolic (boosts), and parabolic (shears), learning the exact Lie algebra generator parameters end-to-end.
-> the invariance/equivariance isn't approximate. it's guaranteed by construction. you can formally trace which symmetry the model discovered and why.
-> validated on tasks like pendulum dynamics and moment of inertia prediction.

paper : https://t.co/jlFDvOVSXw
see you in Seoul🇰🇷!!
